channel_name: Invest Like the Best · Patrick O'Shaughnessy
video_title: The 2028 Natural Gas Crisis No One Sees Coming
gist: An energy investor named Matt argues that after 16-18 months modeling every US gas well, pipeline and data-center project, the combination of committed LNG exports (rising from ~15 to 35 BCF/day) and AI compute demand will drive US working gas storage below all historical lows starting mid-2028, making natural gas price risk 'unbounded and convex' and forcing electricity prices sharply higher into 2028-2030. He is bullish gas producers (Expand Energy, Range) and solar assets, bullish large-scale AP-1000 nuclear as the only long-term fix, and bearish gas-turbine/fuel-cell makers, hyperscalers and the US consumer.

insights.claim: US working gas storage will break materially below all historical lows starting mid-2028 and drop below all known historical storage evidence by 2029, driven by committed LNG exports plus AI compute demand outrunning producible supply.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: A structural (not weather-driven) storage draw would break the flat forward curve; positioning long 2028-2030 gas exposure ahead of consensus is the trade.
insights.quote: When you get to the middle of 2028, we start to break very materially below in a historical way where gas available in storage has ever been before. And by 2029, we drop below all known historical storage evidence.

insights.claim: Natural gas now supplies over 40% of US power generation and has overtaken petroleum as the most important fuel in the country, so gas scarcity transmits directly into electricity prices.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Because gas sets the marginal power price, a gas squeeze is really an electricity-price shock that hits every energy-input business.
insights.quote: Natural gas which is over 40% of US power generation has become or is imminently going to become the most important fuel in the country.

insights.claim: The upside price risk in natural gas is 'unbounded and convex' as the deficit builds into 2028-2030, and the pain is felt most acutely in electricity prices.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Convexity favors owning gas/electricity upside via cheap options or under-priced producer equity rather than linear exposure.
insights.quote: the upside risk price of natural gas is both you know unbounded and convex where you will feel it the most acutely will be electricity prices in 28 29 2030

insights.claim: Historical gas spikes ($8-10/MCF during Russia-Ukraine and $6-10 during polar vortex events) were transitory, whereas this deficit is driven by structural demand against a known production ceiling, so a price move need not mean-revert.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Distinguishing structural from transitory means the trade is a durable re-rating, not a spike to fade.
insights.quote: what we're talking about are structural drivers of demand against a known possible production of gas and they don't match up

themes.theme: Demand: LNG Exports and AI Compute

insights.claim: US LNG export nameplate capacity has grown from near zero to ~15 BCF/day and is contractually scheduled to reach up to 35 BCF/day by end of 2030, on a US production base of ~110-112 BCF/day, making exports 12-15% of daily supply with the incremental demand 'die' already largely cast.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: ~20 BCF/day of new demand is locked in via project-financed LNG contracts before AI is even added, so the demand side is not speculative.
insights.quote: as of today we're scheduled to export uh up to 35 BCF a day by the end of 2030 and in that case the die has been mostly cast

insights.claim: The primary US gas demand driver over the past decade has been LNG exports, not AI; the AI compute pull is only a recent incremental addition on top of an already-committed export ramp.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Investors fixated on data centers are underweighting the LNG base load that makes the system fragile before AI is counted.
insights.quote: the the dye was cast long before AI compute came to the scene

insights.claim: In the base case, credible AI-compute gas demand (P50 assets with approvals, PPAs and interconnection) is ~5 BCF/day, but a P30/P0 unmitigated scenario more than doubles that to 12-15 BCF/day by the early 2030s.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: The gap between base and extreme AI demand (5 vs 12-15 BCF/day) is the swing factor determining how severe the squeeze becomes.
insights.quote: If you start to move it down and say P30 or P 0, that number, you know, more can more than double and be, you know, 12 to 15 BCF a day by the early 2030s if if unmititigated.

insights.claim: Every current power solution for AI (from GE Vernova combined-cycle down to fuel cells and Caterpillar/Solar turbines) consumes more natural gas, and 'bring your own generation' (BYOG) mandates on hyperscalers structurally increase gas demand, not reduce it.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Treat every new power-gen announcement (Bloom, GE Vernova) as a bullish gas-demand signal: 'think more gas.'
insights.quote: every time you read a press release from Bloom or from Genev, think more gas.

insights.claim: A Bloom Energy 6-series fuel cell consumes ~150 million cubic feet of gas per day per gigawatt, and the market prices Bloom at ~2 GW/year of capacity ramping toward 5 GW, for which the gas does not exist unless taken from another use.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Bloom's implied gas draw is a concrete way to size incremental demand and question whether its capacity ramp is physically feasible.
insights.quote: 6 series bloom energy you know kind of latest gen fuel cell will take 150 million cubic feet a day of gas per gawatt

insights.claim: Simply shutting off LNG exports to fix the domestic shortage is impractical because of contract law, tens of billions in project financing, and US exports becoming ~one-third of global gas supply that FTA and non-FTA allies depend on.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: The obvious 'just keep it home' relief valve is largely blocked, meaning the deficit is more binding than a naive analysis assumes.
insights.quote: It's more complicated than that contract law.

themes.theme: Supply Ceiling: Resource, Processing, Gathering, Pipelines

insights.claim: Using digitized acreage polygons and well-level data, maximum US gas deliverability is capped at roughly 128-132 BCF/day even assuming every company optimally drills its remaining captured inventory, and that highest 132 estimate is the number required just to satisfy committed LNG exports.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: A hard ~132 BCF/day flow ceiling against rising committed demand is the crux of the deficit; producers with the best remaining rock are the scarce asset.
insights.quote: you get to this 1328 130 132 BCF a day of of maximum deliverability

insights.claim: The problem is not resource in the ground but deliverability within the time frame: the US gets through most existing captured well inventory in the next four or five years, and incremental supply is throttled by processing, gathering and pipeline constraints, not geology.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Because the bottleneck is midstream and time, high prices alone can't quickly fix supply, extending the squeeze's duration.
insights.quote: we get through most of the existing captured inventory of companies in the next four or five years

insights.claim: Processing capacity (which takes 2-3 years to build) is insufficient to reach the assumed 20 BCF/day production growth, so processing must be built imminently or the supply target is unreachable.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Midstream processing build-out lead times are a real-time indicator of whether the deficit can be avoided; watch announced projects.
insights.quote: You really would need to in the near term even to get to our 20 BCF of incremental gas production we're willing to estimate you need to have more processing built

insights.claim: Only one major interstate gas pipeline (Mountain Valley Pipeline, Appalachia to Mid-Atlantic) has been built in the last 10-12 years due to environmental permitting and regulatory hurdles, making pipeline connectivity a severe rate-limiter on moving gas to AI load.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Pipeline scarcity locks gas away from demand centers; permitting reform progress (or lack of it) is a key catalyst to monitor.
insights.quote: in the last 10 or 12 years we've really built one interstate a gas pipeline that was Mountain Valley pipeline connecting Appalachia to Mid-Atlantic

insights.claim: The Permian 'plenty of gas' rebuttal is already in the base case (7+ BCF/day of pipelines coming 2026-2030), and producing materially more Permian associated gas would require sustained much-higher oil prices, which would only worsen the consumer crisis.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: The bull-case 'Permian saves us' argument fails unless oil stays elevated, so cheap-oil scenarios do not relieve the gas squeeze.
insights.quote: In order to produce a lot more perian gas than even these seven plus BCF pipelines being built that were already modeling, you'd need much much higher oil for longer

insights.claim: The natural gas forward curve is flat at ~$3.50-3.60 out to 2030 because 15 years of abundance has bred complacency, and no real-time indicator (rig count, EQT shutting in gas) shows the market is onto the coming 2028-2030 tightness.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: A flat curve mispricing a structural deficit is the core opportunity; the trade is on before rig counts or hedging reveal the tightness.
insights.quote: gas has lulled everybody to sleep

insights.claim: The 2028 gas market resembles the DRAM/memory shortage: capacity under-investment bites 'slowly at first and then all at once,' and the squeeze is visible six-plus months out, not two years out.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: The memory-shortage analog implies an abrupt repricing; front-running utility hedging is time-sensitive.
insights.quote: imagine being short memory a year ago or or 18 months ago and finding out all of a sudden you're short memory. That is what this natural gas market looks like to us, not two years out, but six plus months out.

insights.claim: The 2028 gas market is currently illiquid, which is why the forward curve hasn't moved; once utilities and gas generators begin hedging and securing physical supply for 2028, a 'knife fight' for physical gas will erupt and forward curves could move up materially within six months.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: The catalyst is utilities rolling forward to hedge 2028; that repricing window is the entry timing.
insights.quote: you're going to start to see a knife fight to secure natural gas physical in 28 like we really haven't seen before

insights.claim: Investors refuse to price near-term gas above appropriately-supplied levels yet will pay for untested 2035 technologies, an inconsistency the speaker finds surprising across the sectors he follows.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: This valuation asymmetry favors owning cheap, near-term cash-generating gas assets over speculative long-dated tech.
insights.quote: investors are not willing to look past near-term appropriate appropriately supplied gas market, but they're willing to pay for something in 2035 that is totally untested or unproven

insights.claim: Expand Energy is the biggest winner, controlling ~70% of remaining core Haynesville wells of known high-productivity rock, yet trades at ~4x forward EBITDA with a low-to-mid teens free cash flow yield after its stock plummeted amid a leadership vacuum and CEO search.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: A dominant Haynesville producer priced on a complacent flat curve offers highly asymmetric upside if the gas thesis plays out.
insights.quote: expand we think is far and away the biggest winner uniquely expand is CEO list right now

insights.claim: Range Resources is the highest-quality upstream company in Appalachia with significant room to grow production and materially grow returns to investors.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Range is a second high-quality producer levered to the gas re-rating with production growth optionality.

insights.claim: Utility-scale solar assets get a windfall from rising electricity prices at zero incremental capital cost because gas sets the marginal power price while sunlight stays free; XPLR (ticker XIFR, formerly NextEra Yield Co) benefits by marking PPAs to market at much higher values in the early 2030s.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Solar yieldcos are a non-obvious long: margin expansion with no capex as gas-set power prices rise; watch PPA repricing schedules.
insights.quote: Solar assets stand to benefit from a windfall where electricity prices are going up because gas is setting the margin of plant taking fuel where the price is increasing is rising while sun costs the same.

insights.claim: Residential solar plus batteries will grow exponentially and become economic even without tax incentives for the first time, as it is the main way households can protect against very high 10am-6pm peak power prices once gas tightens.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: A depressed residential-solar industry could inflect upward on economics alone; contrarian long into a sector left for dead after incentive removal.
insights.quote: We think residential solar grows exponentially from here.

themes.theme: Nuclear as the Only Long-Term Fix

insights.claim: Large-scale nuclear, specifically Westinghouse AP-1000 units, is the only viable long-term solution and must come online by 2033-2034 (the soonest possible), because the gas deficit only worsens in 2031-2032 and beyond and there is no bridge fuel other than solar and wind.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Nuclear build timelines mean commitments must happen now; AP-1000 supply chain names are a multi-year structural long.
insights.quote: the only viable solution is to build large-scale nuclear um as fast as possible, which would mean it needs to come on in 2033 or 2034

insights.claim: Westinghouse is a deeply undervalued asset held inside Cameco (49%) and Brookfield (51%) and will surface value when Westinghouse comes public; the US government appears to be lining up early procurement to derisk the nuclear supply chain.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Cameco offers indirect, underpriced exposure to a Westinghouse re-rating and AP-1000 order cycle.
insights.quote: when the Westinghouse comes public and it's deeply undervalued within Camo today

insights.claim: BWXT, the primary supplier of nuclear components for the US Navy, significantly benefits from the coming nuclear cycle with large dollar content in the AP-1000.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: BWXT is a supply-chain long leveraged to both Navy nuclear and new large-scale reactor builds.

insights.claim: SMRs are largely unviable as the near-term fix because many are still science experiments and are not set up to manufacture and scale to the tens of gigawatts needed by the 2030s, so large-scale reactors win over small.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Fade the SMR hype for scale solutions; capital should favor proven large-scale AP-1000 economics over unscaled SMR bets.
insights.quote: many of these SMR companies are not set up to manufacture and truly scale uh for the solution that's needed

insights.claim: China is currently building ~39 nuclear reactors, 34 of which are 1 GW-plus and about a third modeled after the AP-1000, evidence that large-scale nuclear is buildable on a known timeline when a country commits.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: China's build proves the AP-1000 template scales, supporting the thesis that US large-scale nuclear is a matter of will, not feasibility.
insights.quote: China is building 39 plus or minus nuclear reactors. 34 of them are 1 gawatt plus um I think a third of those are modeled after the AP 1000.

themes.theme: Losers: Consumers, Turbine and Fuel-Cell Makers, Hyperscalers

insights.claim: The US consumer is the biggest loser, facing rising electricity bills and an 'awful trade-off' between exporting gas, powering AI compute, or keeping consumer bills low, likely fueling more NIMBYism and public backlash.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Political backlash over consumer bills is itself a risk to LNG export and data-center buildout policy; a regulatory/political overhang.
insights.quote: the biggest losers of this would be the US consumer

insights.claim: Gas-turbine and distributed power-gen manufacturers (Caterpillar doubling Solar turbine capacity through end of 2029) are adding capacity at the exact wrong time, repeating the early-2000s boom-bust, as expensive gas may make buyers question deploying those assets.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Turbine/genset makers that have had huge stock runs may face an order air-pocket; a contrarian short/underweight into 2028-2030.
insights.quote: Caterpillar is I think they're doubling their uh, solar turbine capacity between now and the end of 29, which I would judge is just at the exact wrong time

insights.claim: Bloom Energy is poorly positioned because its fuel cells at 2 GW-plus scale won't be able to secure natural gas in competition with other assets amid the scarcity, so they are treated only as backup generation in the base case.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Bloom's scale story may be structurally un-fuelable; if deployed as base load it only adds to the convexity, a bearish setup.
insights.quote: we don't think that Bloom Energy's assets at 2 gawatt or more will be able to get natural gas in competition with all of the other assets that are being deployed

insights.claim: Orders for new large-scale natural gas generation could slow very meaningfully starting as 2026 progresses, because it won't make sense to build gas plants after ~2029-2030 until production ramps and deliverability security improves.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: A slowing new-gas-plant order cycle is a leading indicator and a headwind for gas-turbine OEM backlogs.
insights.quote: we we could see orders slow very meaningfully for natural gas generating assets even at large scale as 26 progresses

insights.claim: Energy is budgeted at ~10% of hyperscaler cost today (behind depreciation), but if gas-powered compute doubles or triples structurally, energy could become 20-30-40% of the cost of compute by 2029, threatening hyperscaler profitability.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: A gas-price shock is a material, under-modeled hit to hyperscaler margins; performance-per-watt becomes a key metric to track.
insights.quote: energy is total cost of energy is supposed to be about 10%. If you plug in all of this compute and it's gas powered and we think gas could double or triple structurally even without weather, it could end up being 20 or 30% of the cost of compute by 2029.

insights.claim: EPC/engineering-construction firms building gas plants and trading at ~25x cash flow (a historically high multiple) are exposed if gas-plant builds stall in 2029-2030, and should pursue accretive M&A to diversify away from gas-generation dependence.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: High-multiple gas-EPC names carry undocumented backlog risk; watch for diversifying M&A as a defensive signal.
insights.quote: trading at 25 times cash flow which is a historically high multiple for a engineering construction firm and your main business is building natural gas plants

insights.claim: The single highest-impact government action would be to fully build 2-4 AP-1000 nuclear plants (potentially funding it from the ~$260 billion Loan Program Office budget) to derisk the supply chain and catalyze a broader nuclear buildout, since currently no one wants to go first.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Government first-mover nuclear commitments are the key catalyst that could jumpstart the AP-1000 supply chain; a policy trigger to watch.
insights.quote: I would find a way to build entirely from beginning to end two to four AP-1000 nuclear plants

insights.claim: A 1-2 BCF/day pipeline from Canada into the US Midwest (MISO) could help, since Canada has the deepest, richest economic gas resource but it is trapped behind pipe; currently Canada only supplies ~11-12 BCF seasonally in January and has limited storage (~0.5 TCF, a quarter of the US).
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Cross-border pipeline capacity is a multi-year relief valve; Canadian gas resource and pipeline projects into MISO are worth tracking.
insights.quote: Canada by far has the deepest and richest resource of economic gas in the ground, but it's been trapped behind pipe

insights.claim: If the US cannot deliver its planned 30-35+ BCF/day of LNG, global rebalancing forces Europe to choose between Russian gas or much costlier US gas, while Asia (the largest LNG consumer) is squeezed, potentially harming key allies.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: US LNG curtailment has geopolitical spillover; global gas and European power prices are downstream of US domestic tightness.
insights.quote: you probably impact Europe meaningfully and they're left with a trade-off of taking Russian gas or U or or or much much higher cost US gas

insights.claim: A step-function battery-technology breakthrough (e.g. sodium-ion beyond today's fast-discharge lithium-ion) is the main disruption risk that could invalidate the thesis, and the speaker would welcome it as it would solve the problem.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Battery breakthroughs are the key bear-case risk to gas-long positioning; monitor non-lithium storage commercialization.
insights.quote: step function battery battery technology change could be something that would would affect you

insights.claim: The key challenge for any energy-input business is to lock up physical gas supply now and understand counterparty risk, which will become very meaningful in 2-3 years and has been ignored during the AI boom.
insights.speaker: Matt (guest, energy investor)
insights.so_what: Physical gas provisioning and counterparty exposure are under-priced risks; firms that secured supply early are advantaged.
insights.quote: Make sure you have physical supply locked up and that you understand your counterparties and what will likely be very meaningful counterparty risk in two or three years.
